extracted_claims: Must be Arbitrum, not Ethereum L1.
Yes Max concurrent transcoding sessions.
Default 10 set to your benchmarked limit.
Typical range: 500 2,000.
Check current rates at tools.livepeer.cloud.
Must be reachable from the internet on port 8935.
AI inference configuration AI inference serves inference requests (text to image, audio to text, LLM, and other pipelines) from gateways and applications.
AI routing is capability based no top 100 stake required to receive AI jobs.
docker compose template: Required: create before starting: Check current demand and pricing at before setting .
Must be a host path Docker mounts this into AI runner containers.
Without this mount, AI pipelines will not start.
A 6 GB model like SDXL Lightning takes 5 30 minutes depending on connection speed.
It requires arbETH for gas and LPT for staking.

Choose a provider: Provider Best for Rate limit Alchemy (free tier) Most operators 300M compute units/month Infura (free tier) Most operators 100K requests/day (public) Testing only Rate limited, no uptime guarantee For production nodes, use a hosted provider.
Public endpoints have no uptime guarantees and will drop sessions if they become unavailable a missed connection during round initialisation forfeits that round's LPT reward permanently.
Create an account at 2.
Create a new app select Arbitrum and Mainnet 3.
Copy the HTTPS endpoint: Verify the endpoint responds correctly: Expected: this is Arbitrum One (chain ID 42161).
Record it this is the address that will receive ETH fees and hold staked LPT.
Alternatively, check the current account via : The orchestrator wallet needs ETH on Arbitrum One for gas.
All on chain transactions (activation, reward calls, ticket redemption) consume ETH.
Recommended minimum: 0.05 ETH for initial setup and ongoing gas costs.

How to get arbETH: Buy ETH directly on an exchange that supports Arbitrum One withdrawals (Binance, Coinbase, Kraken) Bridge ETH from Ethereum L1 at (bridge confirmation: 10 15 minutes) Verify the balance on by searching the orchestrator address before proceeding.
LPT must be bonded to the orchestrator address to enter the active set for video transcoding.
Setup covers the technical readiness of the node: installed, configured, staked, and verified.
→ Configure your orchestrator Connect to Arbitrum One: fund the wallet with arbETH, acquire and stake LPT, register the service URI, and activate on chain.
→ Connect and activate Confirm the node is on chain, transcoding video, running AI inference (if configured), calling reward correctly, and exposing metrics.
24 GB VRAM for AI diffusion pipelines; 8 GB for LLM via Ollama.
See the Requirements guide for per pipeline VRAM floors.
Binary install also supported for Linux, Windows, macOS.
Blockchain arbETH + LPT on Arbitrum One ~0.05 ETH for gas.
LPT stake: check current active set threshold on Explorer.
Network Public IP or domain; port 8935 open Gateways connect on port 8935.
Time 2 4 hours for video; 4 8 hours for AI or dual mode Most of the time is LPT acquisition and model download, not active configuration.
How orchestrators earn from service fees and LPT inflation rewards.
Tune pricing, capacity, session limits, and reward calling gas economics.
This page covers the minimum monitoring you should have in place once your orchestrator is live.

Enable Prometheus metrics Add to your go livepeer startup command: Metrics are exposed at: Verify with: Key metrics to watch Metric What it tells you Total segments received — confirms you are getting jobs Successfully transcoded segments Failed transcodes — high values reduce gateway selection probability Time to transcode each segment — should stay well under real time Current Livepeer protocol round Number of reward calls submitted — should increment once per round For AI inference metrics, also watch and .
Check Explorer After each round (~24 hours), verify on : 1.
Status — should show Active 2.
Reward calls — should show a reward for each round your node was online 3.

Fees earned — will appear after you process transcoding or AI jobs If reward calls are missing, check: Your node was online and connected during the round You did not start with Your Arbitrum ETH balance is sufficient for gas GPU monitoring Check GPU health and utilisation: Watch for: Temperature above 85°C — indicates inadequate cooling Memory utilisation near 100% — may cause out of memory errors for AI workloads GPU utilisation at 0% when you should be processing jobs — check go livepeer logs Quick symptom guide Symptom Likely cause Action No segments in logs Service address unreachable or price too high Re check port 8935 from external machine; compare pricing on Explorer Reward calls missing Node offline during round, or Confirm uptime; check startup flags High transcode errors GPU driver issue or CUDA mismatch Run ; check go livepeer error logs RPC errors in logs Arbitrum endpoint down or rate limited Test RPC with ; switch to a different provider if needed AI runner not responding Container crashed or port conflict ; check For detailed troubleshooting, see the .

Hardware Component Minimum Recommended GPU Any NVIDIA with NVENC/NVDEC RTX 3060+ (consumer) or T4+ (datacentre) VRAM 4 GB 8 GB+ CPU 4 cores 8 cores RAM 16 GB 32 GB Storage 50 GB SSD 100 GB NVMe SSD CPU only transcoding is possible (omit the flag) but significantly slower.

Component Minimum Recommended GPU NVIDIA with CUDA support RTX 3090 / 4090 / A100 VRAM 8 GB (LLM only via Ollama) 24 GB+ (diffusion + LLM + audio) CPU 4 cores 8 cores RAM 16 GB 32 GB Storage 100 GB SSD 250 GB+ NVMe SSD (AI model weights) VRAM determines which AI pipelines the orchestrator can serve.
8 GB runs quantised LLMs only.
24 GB+ unlocks diffusion, audio, vision, and real time AI.
See for per model VRAM requirements.

Component Minimum Recommended GPU NVIDIA with NVENC + CUDA RTX 3090 / 4090 (24 GB VRAM) VRAM 16 GB 24 GB+ (one warm AI model + video transcoding) CPU 4 cores 8+ cores RAM 16 GB 32 GB Storage 100 GB SSD 250 GB+ NVMe SSD NVENC/NVDEC (video) use dedicated silicon that does not compete with CUDA cores (AI).
Both workloads share VRAM.
With 24 GB, the orchestrator can run video transcoding and one warm AI model simultaneously.
See for VRAM coexistence details.
Software Requirement Detail Verify OS Linux (Ubuntu 22.04+ recommended).

NVIDIA driver Version 525+ (Linux) or 527+ (Windows) CUDA 12.0+ (or use Docker image which bundles CUDA) Docker (recommended) Docker Engine + NVIDIA Container Toolkit for GPU passthrough Network Requirement Detail Port 8935 TCP Open to the public internet (firewall, router NAT, cloud security group).
Static IP or hostname Required for the service URI that gateways connect to.
Docker is supported on Linux, Windows (WSL2), and macOS.
The official Docker image includes CUDA 12.0 no separate CUDA installation required.
The image must be pulled before starting the node.
Visit and download the archive matching your CPU and use case: Use the archive only when the host has an NVIDIA GPU and matching CUDA 12 libraries installed.
The AI runner container requires Linux and does not run natively on Windows.
In PowerShell: Expected: The AI runner container requires Linux.
macOS GPU transcoding uses the NVIDIA CUDA path, which requires an NVIDIA GPU.
Most modern Macs use Apple Silicon (M series) which is not supported for NVIDIA CUDA transcoding.
It confirms the node is on chain, processing jobs correctly, and exposing the minimum monitoring signals.
Check: Field Expected Action if wrong Status Active (or Registered if activation was recent) Wait one full round (~22 hours).
If still not Active, check stake vs top 100 threshold.
Service URI Matches exactly Update via → Set orchestrator config → service URI.
Stake Reflects bonded LPT amount If stake shows 0, the bond transaction may not have confirmed.
Reward cut / Fee cut Match the values set during activation Adjustable via at any time.
The configured pipelines should appear with the correct warm/cold status.
Test from a different machine (not the same server): Any response (including a JSON error) confirms port 8935 is open and reachable.
For this test to work, a gateway must be routing the RTMP stream to the orchestrator.
If running a standalone orchestrator without a local gateway, skip this test and monitor for messages after the node enters the active set and gateways start routing production traffic.
Also confirm the AI runner container is healthy: If the runner is in a restart loop, check its logs: Reward calling distributes LPT inflation to the orchestrator each round (~22 hours).
Also check the Rewards section on the orchestrator page updates after each successful call.

snippets: This requires: Automated monitoring with immediate alerts on node failure Automated restart and recovery Stable, redundant connectivity (not shared home broadband) Consistent power supply (UPS or colocation) Hardware health monitoring (GPU temperatures, VRAM utilisation) Model warm up management For AI inference workloads, cold model starts (loading a model from disk into VRAM on first request) introduce latency that breaks user facing SLAs.
The practical implication: the VRAM requirements for commercial AI operation are determined by the sum of all models that must be simultaneously loaded, not just the largest single model.

Prioritise: Pipelines with few available Orchestrators and active demand High VRAM models that exclude commodity GPU competition Cascade (real time AI) pipelines if hardware supports it higher per job value Commercial pricing is part market positioning and part relationship management.
This is the natural next step for video Orchestrators who invest in high VRAM GPUs.

Path Best fit today Main constraint Pool worker Any NVIDIA GPU, little or no LPT, lower operating commitment Lower control and lower earnings ceiling than running a full Orchestrator Solo AI Orchestrator 24 GB+ VRAM, limited LPT, willing to manage warm models and demand shifts AI demand, model selection, and pricing change faster than video workloads Solo video Orchestrator Reliable GPU and bandwidth, plus enough LPT to stay competitive for the active set Active set stake is the biggest barrier to receiving steady video work Commercial or pool operator Multiple GPUs or direct Gateway demand, and readiness for 99%+ operational standards Requires relationship building, monitoring, and disciplined pricing What Orchestrators Earn Orchestrators earn from two separate streams.
Batch AI inference: 8 24 GB VRAM depending on pipelines.
An RTX 3090 (24 GB) is the practical minimum for competitive diffusion pipelines.
Real time AI (Cascade): 24 GB VRAM minimum.

8 GB runs quantised LLMs only.
24 GB+ unlocks diffusion, audio, vision, and real time AI.

VRAM planning: NVENC/NVDEC use dedicated silicon they consume negligible VRAM.
The VRAM constraint is set entirely by AI model weights.
On a 24 GB GPU, one warm SDXL Lightning model (~12 18 GB) leaves room for video sessions alongside it.

Most diffusion model pipelines require 16 GB or more.

snippets: Path Best fit today Main constraint Pool worker Any NVIDIA GPU, little or no LPT, lower operating commitment Lower control and lower earnings ceiling than running a full Orchestrator Solo AI Orchestrator 24 GB+ VRAM, limited LPT, willing to manage warm models and demand shifts AI demand, model selection, and pricing change faster than video workloads Solo video Orchestrator Reliable GPU and bandwidth, plus enough LPT to stay competitive for the active set Active set stake is the biggest barrier to receiving steady video work Commercial or pool operator Multiple GPUs or direct Gateway demand, and readiness for 99%+ operational standards Requires relationship building, monitoring, and disciplined pricing What Orchestrators Earn Orchestrators earn from two separate streams.
To receive video transcoding jobs as a solo Orchestrator, the node must be in the active set the top Orchestrators by total bonded stake eligible to receive work each round.
Acquiring enough LPT to compete in the active set is the primary entry barrier for solo video Orchestrators.
A well configured AI node with warm models loaded can compete from day one without active set membership.
That is the core shift in the business case: the upside comes from being part of an application's serving path, not just from holding more bonded LPT.

Practical discipline for commercial capability management: Declare only models that are loaded and warm at startup Remove capability declarations for models that are not being actively served Use to specify exactly which pipeline/model combinations to load on startup Monitor model load times and remove slow start models from the active set Building Gateway relationships Active commercial relationships with Gateways typically develop through: Consistent performance history visible on the Livepeer Explorer Participation in the channel on the Direct outreach to Gateway SPEs and ecosystem partners Demonstrated capability support for pipelines that specific Gateways need Gateways serving AI products actively look for Orchestrators with specific capability profiles.
Amount varies by active set competition.
Video operators need enough for active set membership.

snippets: At a Glance Alternative What it solves What it requires Guide Pool worker Earn from GPU compute through a pool worker path with the operator handling stake and protocol operations GPU; a pool to join; zero stake O T split Separate protocol management from GPU workload processing; scale Transcoder machines independently Dedicated Orchestrator and Transcoder hosts; staked LPT; shared Siphon Keep the keystore on an isolated machine; GPU restarts leave LPT rewards uninterrupted Two machines; staked LPT; Python on secure machine Pool Worker A pool worker runs against an existing pool operator's Orchestrator address.

Choosing the Right Alternative Situation Right path No LPT, just a GPU Pool worker worker only earnings path One machine, comfortable with the single process deployment Standard single process deployment see the Setup Guide for the default architecture Multiple GPU machines under one Orchestrator identity O T split scale Transcoder machines behind a single Orchestrator GPU machine uptime is uncertain; LPT rewards must be protected Siphon reward calling is independent of GPU machine state Keystore must be isolated from the media processing machine Siphon GPU machine never holds the keystore Earn inflation rewards before GPU hardware is ready Siphon (secure machine only) add GPU machine later without re registration Running a pool for external workers O T split as the foundation then see Related Pages Contribute GPU compute through an operator managed pool path with no staking or on chain operations.
Pool operators manage the Orchestrator registration, on chain staking, and reward calling for a fleet of GPU workers.

Realistic time commitments: Initial setup and configuration: 4 16 hours (varies by path) Monitoring and maintenance: 1 3 hours per week once stable Troubleshooting: variable go livepeer updates: 30 60 minutes per release Pool workers have minimal ongoing time investment once the worker process is configured.
Solo Orchestrators require consistent monitoring, particularly for reward call reliability and response to Gateway selection issues.

snippets: Revenue stream How it works Depends on ETH job fees Gateways pay per transcoding segment or AI inference job via probabilistic micropayment tickets redeemed on Arbitrum Capability, pricing, performance, uptime, Gateway selection LPT inflation rewards Protocol mints LPT each round (~22 hours); active Orchestrators claim their share by calling once per round Total bonded stake (self + delegated), inflation rate, reward call reliability Neither stream pays out simply because the software is running.
Solo video Orchestrator NVIDIA GPU + reliable 100 Mbps+ Active set threshold High (8 16 hours) Operators who can acquire LPT and want full control plus LPT inflation rewards.

Dimension Hobbyist operator Commercial operator Primary income LPT inflation rewards ETH service fees from job processing Revenue lever Total bonded stake Job volume, pricing discipline, uptime Uptime requirement ~95% (reward calls + basic availability) 99%+ (SLA driven, continuous monitoring) Gateway relationship Passive wait to be selected by price and stake Active direct relationships, per Gateway pricing Hardware approach Existing hardware repurposed Dedicated investment, redundancy planned Monitoring Periodic checks, Prometheus optional Automated alerting, SLA dashboards Model loading Load on demand; cold starts acceptable Pre loaded and warm; cold starts are SLA failures Neither model is inherently better.
Why Service Fees Scale For commercial operators, ETH service fees matter more because they scale with traffic rather than with stake alone.
Most commercial operators treat inflation as a base and fees as the variable upside.

snippets: A GPU machine reboot mid round permanently loses that round's LPT inflation reward, and missed rewards compound over time.
The Siphon split setup solves both.
OrchestratorSiphon runs on a small, secure machine and handles all on chain actions: reward calling, fee withdrawal, governance voting, and service URI updates.

Architecture Machine What it runs GPU required Secure machine OrchestratorSiphon keystore, reward calling, fee withdrawal, on chain actions No (a small VPS is sufficient) GPU machine go livepeer in transcoder mode segment processing, AI inference Yes When to Use This Setup GPU machine uptime is uncertain; keystore isolation from workload processing is needed; multiple GPU machines should run behind one Orchestrator identity; or LPT inflation rewards are needed before GPU infrastructure is ready.
The pool operator handles staking, reward calling, pricing, and Gateway relationships.
The Orchestrator process handles protocol operations, routing, reward calling, and keystore access on one machine.
This lightweight Python tool handles on chain operations only: reward calling, fee withdrawal, governance voting, and service URI updates.

Choosing the Right Alternative Situation Right path No LPT, just a GPU Pool worker worker only earnings path One machine, comfortable with the single process deployment Standard single process deployment see the Setup Guide for the default architecture Multiple GPU machines under one Orchestrator identity O T split scale Transcoder machines behind a single Orchestrator GPU machine uptime is uncertain; LPT rewards must be protected Siphon reward calling is independent of GPU machine state Keystore must be isolated from the media processing machine Siphon GPU machine never holds the keystore Earn inflation rewards before GPU hardware is ready Siphon (secure machine only) add GPU machine later without re registration Running a pool for external workers O T split as the foundation then see Related Pages Contribute GPU compute through an operator managed pool path with no staking or on chain operations.
The split setup separates them: one machine handles protocol operations (on chain interactions, job routing, reward calling) and one or more machines handle the GPU work.
In a correctly configured split setup, Transcoder machines stay keystore free and run with workload only flags.
Combine the split architecture with OrchestratorSiphon for keystore isolation and reward safety.
The pool operator handles staking, on chain registration, reward calling, and routing.

Pool vs solo orchestrator Pool worker Solo orchestrator LPT stake required No Yes On chain registration No (pool handles this) Yes Set your own prices No Yes Direct on chain payouts No (via pool operator) Yes Ops complexity Low Medium High Trust requirement Pool operator Yourself Reward calling responsibility Pool operator You (or Siphon) Good for GPU owners wanting passive income Operators wanting full control Step 1: Choose a pool The Livepeer community currently has a small number of active public pools.
The pool operator handles staking, on chain registration, reward calling, and job routing.

The pool operator handles staking, reward calling, pricing, and Gateway relationships.

snippets: At a Glance Alternative What it solves What it requires Guide Pool worker Earn from GPU compute through a pool worker path with the operator handling stake and protocol operations GPU; a pool to join; zero stake O T split Separate protocol management from GPU workload processing; scale Transcoder machines independently Dedicated Orchestrator and Transcoder hosts; staked LPT; shared Siphon Keep the keystore on an isolated machine; GPU restarts leave LPT rewards uninterrupted Two machines; staked LPT; Python on secure machine Pool Worker A pool worker runs against an existing pool operator's Orchestrator address.
Siphon The Siphon setup also uses a secure machine and a GPU machine, but the secure side runs OrchestratorSiphon.
With Siphon, the secure machine calls rewards independently of the GPU machine state.

Other differences from the single process deployment: Secure machine runs OrchestratorSiphon (Python, ) GPU machine runs with workload processing only Secure machine needs outbound access to Arbitrum RPC and keeps port 8935 closed Siphon runs on the secure machine alone to keep claiming LPT inflation rewards while GPU infrastructure is being set up.
The Siphon split setup solves both.
OrchestratorSiphon runs on a small, secure machine and handles all on chain actions: reward calling, fee withdrawal, governance voting, and service URI updates.

Architecture Machine What it runs GPU required Secure machine OrchestratorSiphon keystore, reward calling, fee withdrawal, on chain actions No (a small VPS is sufficient) GPU machine go livepeer in transcoder mode segment processing, AI inference Yes When to Use This Setup GPU machine uptime is uncertain; keystore isolation from workload processing is needed; multiple GPU machines should run behind one Orchestrator identity; or LPT inflation rewards are needed before GPU infrastructure is ready.
Siphon runs on the secure machine alone to keep claiming LPT inflation rewards while GPU infrastructure is being prepared.
The split setup separates them: one machine handles protocol operations (on chain interactions, job routing, reward calling) and one or more machines handle the GPU work.
In a correctly configured split setup, Transcoder machines stay keystore free and run with workload only flags.
Combine the split architecture with OrchestratorSiphon for keystore isolation and reward safety.

snippets: When jobs are arriving at the Orchestrator and the Transcoder still stays idle: Check whether the Transcoder's capacity is already reported as fully used Check the Orchestrator metrics endpoint An idle Orchestrator usually points to Gateway routing see The Transcoder's GPU startup test failed, typically because the NVENC session cap has been reached on that GPU.

time_sensitive_claims.notes: The current docs already carry REVIEW notes for Ada Lovelace uncertainty. The runner should treat these figures as volatile operational claims, not settled constants.

time_sensitive_claims.summary: Business-case guidance should explicitly stay approximate and local-context dependent. Payback, electricity cost, and 'worth it' framing are useful, but they should not be presented as portable constants across operators, geographies, or market conditions.

snippets: Running a Livepeer Orchestrator is only worth it when your hardware, stake access, and operating tolerance line up with a realistic way to earn.
It breaks the choice into costs, revenue sources, and a small set of viability checks so you can decide whether to stop, start small, or commit to a full Orchestrator path.
The rest of this page explains the cost model, viability checks, and trade offs behind each path.
Three Viability Questions These three questions are the fastest way to rule a path in or out before you spend more time on setup.

time_sensitive_claims.notes: This family is about keeping 'worth it' or break-even style guidance honest and local to the operator's hardware, energy, and stake context.

time_sensitive_claims.summary: Reward-call profitability guidance should remain explicitly time-sensitive because gas cost, ETH price, and LPT price move. Docs must not present rough thresholds as durable constants.

snippets: Is reward calling profitable?
If stake is small, the LPT earned per round may be worth less than the gas cost.

A rough check: open and find the Orchestrator's "Estimated Reward This Round." If that LPT value (converted to ETH at the current price) is less than approximately 0.00005 ETH, the gas cost exceeds the reward at typical Arbitrum rates.
Read this page to tune automatic and manual calls, Arbitrum gas costs, commission updates, and the fee path from job to wallet.
Calling Reward() — your options Option 1: Automatic (default, recommended) By default, go livepeer automatically calls at the start of each new round.

Option 2: Disable automatic, call manually Use this approach when you are newly activated with very low stake, and want to evaluate whether the gas cost exceeds the LPT value before committing to automatic calling.
Step 1: Disable automatic reward calls Step 2: Estimate whether calling is profitable this round Gather three inputs: 1.
Gas costs for voting Voting is an Arbitrum transaction.

time_sensitive_claims.notes: Surface rough thresholds as review-sensitive even when the docs provide a range.

pages.snippet: This requires: Automated monitoring with immediate alerts on node failure Automated restart and recovery Stable, redundant connectivity (not shared home broadband) Consistent power supply (UPS or colocation) Hardware health monitoring (GPU temperatures, VRAM utilisation) Model warm up management For AI inference workloads, cold model starts (loading a model from disk into VRAM on first request) introduce latency that breaks user facing SLAs.

Path Best fit today Main constraint Pool worker Any NVIDIA GPU, little or no LPT, lower operating commitment Lower control and lower earnings ceiling than running a full Orchestrator Solo AI Orchestrator 24 GB+ VRAM, limited LPT, willing to manage warm models and demand shifts AI demand, model selection, and pricing change faster than video workloads Solo video Orchestrator Reliable GPU and bandwidth, plus enough LPT to stay competitive for the active set Active set stake is the biggest barrier to receiving steady video work Commercial or pool operator Multiple GPUs or direct Gateway demand, and readiness for 99%+ operational standards Requires relationship building, monitoring, and disciplined pricing What Orchestrators Earn Orchestrators earn from two separate streams.

Hardware Component Minimum Recommended GPU Any NVIDIA with NVENC/NVDEC RTX 3060+ (consumer) or T4+ (datacentre) VRAM 4 GB 8 GB+ CPU 4 cores 8 cores RAM 16 GB 32 GB Storage 50 GB SSD 100 GB NVMe SSD CPU only transcoding is possible (omit the flag) but significantly slower.
For the LLM pipeline (8 16 GB VRAM): the pipeline uses the Ollama runner instead of .

Video only AI only Dual Mode Revenue streams ETH transcoding fees + LPT rewards ETH AI inference fees Both streams simultaneously Pricing config (wei per pixel) per pipeline Both and GPU execution path NVENC/NVDEC hardware blocks CUDA compute cores Both paths, independent queues VRAM requirement Minimal (frame buffers only) 8 24 GB depending on model 16 GB recommended; 8 GB viable for LLM only pipelines Active set Required for video job eligibility AI job eligibility routes through capability advertisement Required for video; AI routes via capability advertisement OS support Linux, Windows Linux only Linux only Before You Start Confirm these prerequisites before starting: Hardware: NVIDIA GPU with 16 GB VRAM or more.

cross_page_contradictions.best_known_truth: Docs disagree on the practical VRAM minimum for batch AI and real-time AI workloads. Hardware and setup pages should not present conflicting minimums as equally current.

pages.snippet: Path Best fit today Main constraint Pool worker Any NVIDIA GPU, little or no LPT, lower operating commitment Lower control and lower earnings ceiling than running a full Orchestrator Solo AI Orchestrator 24 GB+ VRAM, limited LPT, willing to manage warm models and demand shifts AI demand, model selection, and pricing change faster than video workloads Solo video Orchestrator Reliable GPU and bandwidth, plus enough LPT to stay competitive for the active set Active set stake is the biggest barrier to receiving steady video work Commercial or pool operator Multiple GPUs or direct Gateway demand, and readiness for 99%+ operational standards Requires relationship building, monitoring, and disciplined pricing What Orchestrators Earn Orchestrators earn from two separate streams.
That is the core shift in the business case: the upside comes from being part of an application's serving path, not just from holding more bonded LPT.
Amount varies by active set competition.

cross_page_contradictions.best_known_truth: Solo transcoding guidance repeatedly states that operators need enough bonded LPT to enter the active set, often phrased as top 100, but that wording is time-sensitive and should not be treated as durable fact without current evidence.

cross_page_contradictions.best_known_truth: Pool workers and solo operators have different prerequisites. Pages should not imply that LPT, ETH, or public service exposure are universal requirements for every orchestrator entry path.

pages.snippet: Revenue stream How it works Depends on ETH job fees Gateways pay per transcoding segment or AI inference job via probabilistic micropayment tickets redeemed on Arbitrum Capability, pricing, performance, uptime, Gateway selection LPT inflation rewards Protocol mints LPT each round (~22 hours); active Orchestrators claim their share by calling once per round Total bonded stake (self + delegated), inflation rate, reward call reliability Neither stream pays out simply because the software is running.

Dimension Hobbyist operator Commercial operator Primary income LPT inflation rewards ETH service fees from job processing Revenue lever Total bonded stake Job volume, pricing discipline, uptime Uptime requirement ~95% (reward calls + basic availability) 99%+ (SLA driven, continuous monitoring) Gateway relationship Passive wait to be selected by price and stake Active direct relationships, per Gateway pricing Hardware approach Existing hardware repurposed Dedicated investment, redundancy planned Monitoring Periodic checks, Prometheus optional Automated alerting, SLA dashboards Model loading Load on demand; cold starts acceptable Pre loaded and warm; cold starts are SLA failures Neither model is inherently better.

cross_page_contradictions.best_known_truth: Operator economics pages should keep the two revenue streams aligned: ETH service fees scale with workload and gateway demand, while LPT inflation rewards scale with stake and reward-call reliability. Docs should not flatten those into one generic earnings model.

values: Stake plays a much smaller role
pages.snippet: Batch AI inference AI jobs route on capability match + price ceiling.
Capability, pricing, and latency matter more than active set position for many AI jobs.

cross_page_contradictions.best_known_truth: AI jobs are routed primarily by capability match and price ceilings rather than by stake weight. Docs should not collapse AI routing into the same rules as transcoding routing.

contradictions.best_known_truth: Split-setup guidance depends on OrchestratorSiphon still being an active community-maintained project. That claim should be checked against the current GitHub repository, not just repeated in docs.

unresolved_items.summary: Docs disagree on the practical VRAM minimum for batch AI and real-time AI workloads. Hardware and setup pages should not present conflicting minimums as equally current.

Solo transcoding guidance repeatedly states that operators need enough bonded LPT to enter the active set, often phrased as top 100, but that wording is time-sensitive and should not be treated as durable fact without current evidence.
Pool workers and solo operators have different prerequisites. Pages should not imply that LPT, ETH, or public service exposure are universal requirements for every orchestrator entry path.

Operator economics pages should keep the two revenue streams aligned: ETH service fees scale with workload and gateway demand, while LPT inflation rewards scale with stake and reward-call reliability. Docs should not flatten those into one generic earnings model.
AI jobs are routed primarily by capability match and price ceilings rather than by stake weight. Docs should not collapse AI routing into the same rules as transcoding routing.
A split setup with OrchestratorSiphon isolates the keystore from the GPU machine and keeps reward calling independent of GPU uptime. Docs should keep that claim aligned across setup-path pages.
Split-setup guidance depends on OrchestratorSiphon still being an active community-maintained project. That claim should be checked against the current GitHub repository, not just repeated in docs.

The default session limit and overload behavior are operational facts that multiple setup and hardware pages depend on. When docs state the default cap or the error condition, they should stay aligned with current go-livepeer behavior.

Bandwidth planning for transcoding is currently presented as an approximate planning rule rather than a single durable network constant. Pages should make the source-resolution assumption and rendition-set basis explicit when quoting per-stream bandwidth.

Consumer NVIDIA NVENC session-cap guidance is factual but volatile. Hardware requirement and transcoding guides should stay aligned on whether GeForce-class cards are typically capped at 2-3 concurrent sessions and whether newer generations changed that default.

Business-case guidance should explicitly stay approximate and local-context dependent. Payback, electricity cost, and 'worth it' framing are useful, but they should not be presented as portable constants across operators, geographies, or market conditions.
Reward-call profitability guidance should remain explicitly time-sensitive because gas cost, ETH price, and LPT price move. Docs must not present rough thresholds as durable constants.
